reasoning: Confirmed. The admin_notices callback (lines 221-226) checks isset($_GET['shfs_dismiss_notice']) and calls delete_option('shfs_script_access_level') with no nonce and no current_user_can() check. The phpcs:ignore WordPress.Security.NonceVerification.Recommended comment on line 225 explicitly acknowledges the missing nonce. The hook runs on every wp-admin page load, so any authenticated user in the admin context (not unauthenticated) can trigger it. A CSRF vector exists: an img src pointing to any wp-admin URL with ?shfs_dismiss_notice=true will silently delete the option on behalf of any admin who loads the attacker-controlled page. The outer gate (get_option('shfs_script_access_level')) must be truthy, which is only true on sites that previously used the legacy plugin version and have not yet dismissed the migration notice. The effect is confined to suppressing the one-time migration warning notice that prompts admins to review author/contributor script access settings; it does not directly alter the actual permission options (jamify_hfs_allow_author, jamify_hfs_allow_contributor). No sanitizer or guard was missed. Severity is correctly rated low: the impact is indirect (an admin may not see the security-review reminder), not a direct privilege escalation or data exposure.
